title: How investigators track the causes of wildfires: 'A snail shell and a stone are valuable clues'
subhead: Firefighters, police and forest agents are searching for the ignition point of the major fire in southwestern France, much as they would at a crime scene.

Emmanuel Grégoire, an omnipresent Paris mayor with a centralized governance
subhead: In his first few months, the new Socialist mayor has broken with Anne Hidalgo's remote style, micromanaging every portfolio. His grip is starting to test the patience of allies.

subhead: The deals that US President Donald Trump so readily boasts about seemed destined to get bogged down in the Middle East right from the start.

title: 'I would do a line before service, hidden in the bathroom': How cocaine is flooding the restaurant industry
subhead: Cocaine at work (2/3). From fast-food joints to Michelin-starred restaurants, drug use has become as commonplace as it is alarming in an industry driven by the pursuit of performance, a toxic party culture and grueling working conditions.

title: 'Thank you, dear Hungary, for proving that anything is always possible'
subhead: Jean-Baptiste Chastand, Le Monde's Central Europe correspondent, writes a letter to this 'hostile and surprising' country he has covered for more than 10 years.

'We were meant to take as many jewels as we could': The confessions of two Louvre burglars
subhead: After months of silence, two men suspected of stealing the royal jewels described their involvement in detail during new interrogations in June. According to their statements, they were hired to carry out the heist for '€15,000 to €25,000.'

title: Martine Barrat, a photographer without fear or limits
subhead: At 93, the artist has lost none of her vitality or curiosity. She lives at the legendary Hotel Chelsea in New York, where she remains one of the last year-round artist residents. Today, her work is the subject of a retrospective at the Rencontres d'Arles photography festival.

title: 'If they catch me, it's over for me': Senegal's LGBTQ+ community lives in daily fear of repression
subhead: In March, Senegal passed a law increasing penalties for same-sex relations, leaving LGBTQ+ people living in fear. Several described their deeply uncertain daily lives, gripped by the constant anxiety of being arrested.
